one_line_takeaway: SafeGPT combines input-side pattern/NER/knowledge-graph detection with graduated redaction and output-side moderation for accidental (non-adversarial) enterprise data leakage, and its own ablation shows the deterministic pattern-matching stage -- not the semantic NER/KG components -- does essentially all the recall-critical work on structured-secret leakage, while the knowledge-graph-driven proprietary-term flagging trades a 78.6% false-positive rate on EnterpriseScenarios for conservative IP protection.
key_finding: On PIIBench, SafeGPT reaches 100.0% precision / 70.0% recall / 82.4 F1 / 0.0% FPR (18 leakages) vs Regex-Only's 100.0%/66.7%/80.0/0.0% (20 leakages); Simple NER fails entirely (0%/0%, 60 leakages) because it cannot catch structured patterns; Keyword Blocking gets 40.0% recall (36 leakages); the naive Hybrid (Regex+NER) exactly matches Regex-Only (66.7% recall, 20 leakages), which the paper interprets as evidence that naive combination provides no benefit. On ToxicChat, SafeGPT gets perfect 100/100/100/0.0% while every baseline scores 0% across the board (45 leakages each), attributed to policy violations requiring semantic understanding that only SafeGPT's output-side stage provides. On EnterpriseScenarios, SafeGPT gets 68.2% recall but only 40.5% precision and a 78.6% FPR -- WORSE precision than Regex-Only (51.7%) -- because the knowledge-graph component aggressively flags proprietary terms even in benign contexts; the paper frames this FPR as a 'deliberate over-blocking preference for IP protection' rather than a flaw. The ablation on PIIBench shows pattern matching is the dominant contributor: removing it drops recall by 55 percentage points (70.0%->15.0%, leakages 18->51), while removing NER or the knowledge graph has zero effect on PIIBench metrics (identical 70.0% recall/18 leakages), and Output-Only alone matches the w/o-Pattern degradation (15.0% recall, 51 leakages), confirming input-side pattern detection is necessary and cannot be substituted by output-side moderation alone. Headline aggregate numbers stated in the Abstract/Conclusion: 92% precision, 87% recall, <12% false positives (vs DLP's 68% precision, 24% false positives), 84% of output violations remediated, feedback reduces false positives by 34% monthly, end-to-end testing shows zero leakage and 91% compliance vs 23 incidents/47% compliance unguarded, and 4.0+/5.0 user satisfaction -- none of these aggregate figures are broken out in Table 1/Table 2 or otherwise traced to a specific experiment in the visible body text; they appear to be summary claims not directly reproduced in the per-dataset tables shown.
core_contribution: SafeGPT is a two-sided guardrail system for enterprise LLM deployments that adds preventive controls on BOTH interaction boundaries: (1) an input-side guardrail that runs a three-stage hybrid pipeline -- lightweight pattern matching for structured secrets (API keys, tokens, credit card numbers, SSNs), contextual NER fine-tuned on enterprise data for unstructured sensitive entities (e.g. internal project names, customer identifiers), and semantic similarity matching against an enterprise knowledge graph for implicit/proprietary leakage risk -- feeding into graduated enforcement policies (block high-risk, warn+confirm medium-risk, auto-redact low-risk with placeholder tokens like [REDACTED:PROJECT_CODE]); and (2) an output-side guardrail using parallel classifiers for bias/harmful-content detection, policy-compliance checks (e.g. healthcare privacy, financial disclosure), and optional factual-consistency checks, with automated remediation (rephrasing/regeneration) escalating to human review only on remediation failure. A human-in-the-loop feedback loop uses uncertainty-based active learning to periodically retrain thresholds/classifiers from false positives and false negatives. The paper frames this as filling a gap versus prior systems that address only input OR output protection, not both.
threat_model: The paper explicitly distinguishes its threat model from adversarial jailbreak/extraction research: it targets ACCIDENTAL data leakage by employees during legitimate enterprise workflows (e.g. an engineer pasting a full error log containing a live production API key while debugging a 401 error), not deliberate adversarial prompting (DAN-style role-play, gradient-optimized suffixes, prompt injection), which the Related Work section attributes to a 'distinct threat model' studied by prior adversarial-robustness work (Zou et al. 2023; Ganguli et al. 2022; Meng et al. 2025a; Liu et al. 2025a). Risk categories covered: PII disclosure, credential/secret leakage, proprietary/IP exposure via internal project references, and downstream generation of biased/toxic/policy-violating (e.g. discriminatory, age-coded) output, framed against enterprise compliance regimes (GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) evaluation is primarily on SYNTHETIC datasets designed to simulate enterprise risk, which may not capture real-world organizational workflow diversity/ambiguity; (2) the conservative enforcement strategy increases false positives, particularly for proprietary/contextual references (borne out directly by the 78.6% FPR on EnterpriseScenarios); (3) knowledge-graph quality/coverage depends on organizational curation and may vary by domain; (4) the paper does NOT evaluate long-term deployment factors -- user adaptation over time, latency at scale, or adversarial attempts to bypass the guardrails (i.e., no red-teaming of SafeGPT itself is performed, despite the system being explicitly framed against a non-adversarial threat model). Additional issues visible from the text itself but not flagged by the authors as limitations: the datasets (PIIBench, ToxicChat, EnterpriseScenarios) are self-constructed by the authors with no external validation or public release described; sample sizes are small enough that leakage counts are reported in single/double digits (e.g. 18, 20, 45, 51), so percentage differences of a few points correspond to only 1-3 examples; and several headline numbers in the Abstract/Conclusion (92% precision, 87% recall, 84% remediation, 34% monthly FP reduction, 91% vs 47% compliance) are not traceable to any table or experiment section visible in the main text (through page 9 / Appendix C), raising a grounding gap between the paper's summary claims and its shown per-dataset results.

datasets_used: PIIBench (synthetic, ~60% unsafe/40% safe PII-disclosure prompts, authors' own construction)
ToxicChat (synthetic, ~60% unsafe/40% safe policy-violation examples, authors' own construction -- distinct from the public ToxicChat benchmark; not stated to be the same dataset)
EnterpriseScenarios (synthetic, near-balanced healthcare/finance/proprietary-context mix, authors' own construction)

Abstract/Conclusion aggregate claims (not traced to a specific table in the visible text): 92% precision, 87% recall, <12% false positives vs DLP baseline 68% precision/24% false positives; output filter remediates 84% of violations; feedback reduces false positives by 34% monthly; end-to-end zero leakage and 91% compliance vs 23 incidents/47% compliance unguarded; 4.0+/5.0 satisfaction
